(born September 26, 1967) is an American television director and executive producer. Toye began his career as a production assistant for five years and an editor for 15 years, before becoming a television director.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is a frequent collaborator of [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"). He has directed, edited, and produced several episodes of the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") series *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")*,[[2]](#cite_note-2) as well as serving as a producer and director on the [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Network "Fox Broadcasting Network") science fiction series *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")*. In 2024, he won an [Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Directing_for_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing for a Drama Series") for his work on the first season of the [FX](/wiki/FX_(TV_channel) "FX (TV channel)") series *[Shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun_(2024_TV_series) "Shōgun (2024 TV series)")*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Fred Toye](/w/index.php?title=Fred_Toye&redirect=no "Fred Toye"))

American television director, editor and producer

O. Toye"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Frederick+E.+O.+Toye%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Frederick+E.+O.+Toye%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Frederick+E.+O.+Toye%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Frederick+E.+O.+Toye%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Frederick+E.+O.+Toye%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Frederick+E.+O.+Toye%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(March 2012)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Frederick E. O. Toye | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | (1967-09-26) September 26, 1967 (age 58)  [Los Angeles, California](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California") |
| Alma mater | [University of California, Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara") |
| Occupations | Television director, editor, producer |
| Years active | 1989–present |

**Frederick E. O. Toye** (born September 26, 1967) is an American television director and executive producer. Toye began his career as a production assistant for five years and an editor for 15 years, before becoming a television director.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is a frequent collaborator of [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"). He has directed, edited, and produced several episodes of the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") series *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")*,[[2]](#cite_note-2) as well as serving as a producer and director on the [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Network "Fox Broadcasting Network") science fiction series *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")*. In 2024, he won an [Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Directing_for_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing for a Drama Series") for his work on the first season of the [FX](/wiki/FX_(TV_channel) "FX (TV channel)") series *[Shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun_(2024_TV_series) "Shōgun (2024 TV series)")*.

He has also directed episodes of *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)")*, *[Ghost Whisperer](/wiki/Ghost_Whisperer "Ghost Whisperer")*, *[Brothers & Sisters](/wiki/Brothers_%26_Sisters_(2006_TV_series) "Brothers & Sisters (2006 TV series)")*, *[The 4400](/wiki/The_4400 "The 4400")*, *[Moonlight](/wiki/Moonlight_(American_TV_series) "Moonlight (American TV series)")*, *[V](/wiki/V_(2009_TV_series) "V (2009 TV series)")*, *[CSI: NY](/wiki/CSI:_NY "CSI: NY")*, *[Chuck](/wiki/Chuck_(TV_series) "Chuck (TV series)")*, *[The Good Wife](/wiki/The_Good_Wife "The Good Wife")*, *[Falling Skies](/wiki/Falling_Skies "Falling Skies")*, *[Person of Interest](/wiki/Person_of_Interest_(TV_series) "Person of Interest (TV series)")*, *[Fallout](/wiki/Fallout_(American_TV_series) "Fallout (American TV series)")* and other series.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frederick_E._O._Toye&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Toye attended the [University of California, Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara") and graduated with a B.A.[[3]](#cite_note-IM-3) He is a great-grandson of the opera singer [Marguerite Namara](/wiki/Marguerite_Namara "Marguerite Namara").

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frederick_E._O._Toye&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Toye made the move into television directing after working for five years as a production assistant and a decade as an editor. This background in the editing room gave him a solid sense of how to tell a clear story, which helped considerably in his work on such complex series as *[Chuck](/wiki/Chuck_(TV_series) "Chuck (TV series)")* (NBC 2007–12) and *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")* (Fox 2008–13). Los Angeles native Toye got his first screen credit working as a production assistant on Billy Crystal's 1989 HBO special *Midnight Train to Moscow*, before moving into visual effects on films like *[The Addams Family](/wiki/The_Addams_Family_(1991_film) "The Addams Family (1991 film)")* (1991) and then working as an assistant editor on hit films such as *[Forrest Gump](/wiki/Forrest_Gump "Forrest Gump")* (1994) and *[Men in Black](/wiki/Men_in_Black_(1997_film) "Men in Black (1997 film)")* (1997). [[4]](#cite_note-4)

As a TV director, Toye cut his teeth on network TV action and sci-fi thrillers like *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")*, *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(TV_series) "Lost (TV series)")*, and *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")* and has also worked on character-rich dramas such as *[The Good Wife](/wiki/The_Good_Wife "The Good Wife")*. That experience running the gamut between drama genres served Toye well in recent years, directing episodes of genre series like *[Westworld](/wiki/Westworld_(TV_series) "Westworld (TV series)")*, *[Snowpiercer](/wiki/Snowpiercer_(TV_series) "Snowpiercer (TV series)")*, *[Watchmen](/wiki/Watchmen_(TV_series) "Watchmen (TV series)")* and *[The Walking Dead](/wiki/The_Walking_Dead_(TV_series) "The Walking Dead (TV series)")*. In 2024, his prolific output included *[The Boys](/wiki/The_Boys_(TV_series) "The Boys (TV series)")* and *[Fallout](/wiki/Fallout_(American_TV_series) "Fallout (American TV series)")*. The same year, Toye received acclaim for his work on [*Shōgun*](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun_(2024_TV_series) "Shōgun (2024 TV series)"), helming four episodes of the Emmy-winning FX drama series, including season finale "A Dream of a Dream." The season's penultimate episode, "Crimson Sky," earned Toye his first-ever Emmy for outstanding directing, which was also Toye's first-ever career nomination in the category. Toye followed it up by directing the pilot episode of Prime Video’s prequel series *[The Terminal List: Dark Wolf](/wiki/The_Terminal_List:_Dark_Wolf "The Terminal List: Dark Wolf")*, starring Chris Pratt and Taylor Kitsch.[[5]](#cite_note-5) He set to direct the opening episodes of the Prime Video series *[Bloodaxe](/wiki/Bloodaxe_(TV_series) "Bloodaxe (TV series)")* and *[God of War](/wiki/God_of_War_(American_TV_series) "God of War (American TV series)")*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
